title: Hybrid web app using VMware Ubuntu and AWS RDS

tasks.title: Task 1: Sign in to AWS Management Console

steps.text: Click on the Open Console button, and you will get redirected to AWS Console in a new browser tab.

On the AWS sign-in page, Leave the Account ID as default. Never edit/remove the 12-digit Account ID present in the AWS Console. Otherwise, you cannot proceed with the lab. Now copy your Username and Password in the Lab Console to the IAM Username and Password in AWS Console and click on the Sign in button.
Leave the Account ID as default. Never edit/remove the 12-digit Account ID present in the AWS Console. Otherwise, you cannot proceed with the lab.
Now copy your Username and Password in the Lab Console to the IAM Username and Password in AWS Console and click on the Sign in button.
Once Signed In to the AWS Management Console, Make the default AWS Region as US East (N. Virginia) us-east-1.

Execute this command to install the packages of apache server and MySQL:
Install the required tools for the VM:
Enable the apache and verify the status
Task 2: Install the required packages in the VM

tasks.title: Task 3: Enable the port and firewall with SSH

steps.text: Execute this command one-by-one to enable the port for Public access to the RDS with Port 80 and 443 allowed
Task 3: Enable the port and firewall with SSH

apache2 — web server, php + libapache2-mod-php + php-mysql — small server-side runtime and MySQL driver (we use PHP for DB connection only),default-mysql-client — mysql CLI for testing RDS, curl, netcat-openbsd (nc) — debug connectivity, ufw — uncomplicated firewall.

tasks.title: Task 4: Create RDS in AWS console

steps.text: In this task, we create Amazon RDS instance with MySQL database to connect with Local VM’s frontend
Step 1:
Go to AWS console and in the Search bar type “RDS” and select the Aurora and RDS
Step 2:
Click Create a database button
Task 4: Create RDS in AWS console
Step 3:
Select “Standard create” in the database creation method, for the Engine options select the “MySQL”
Task 4: Create RDS in AWS console
Step 4:
For the templates select Sandbox and in default RDS goes Single-AZ in the deployment options.
Step 5:
For the DB instance identifier, type the name 'db-hybrid-host'
Master username - “admin” which is used for login to the DB instance
In the credentials management tab, select the Self-managed to make our own password for this instance.
Type the strong password for the database and remember to write it down by giving the confirmed master password.
Step 6:
In the Instance configuration, in the DB instance class - Select “Burstable classes (includes t classes)”.
In the dropdown, make sure to select the db.t3.micro series instance type, which is enough for our database IOPS.
Task 4: Create RDS in AWS console
Step 7:
Select the storage type General Purpose SSD (gp-2)
Set the allocated storage - “20” Gib only
Uncheck the 'Enable storage autoscaling' in the Additional storage configuration.
Task 4: Create RDS in AWS console
Step 8:
In the connectivity tab,
Select “Don’t connect to an EC2 compute instance”, thus we are not creating any EC2 instance resources.
Select the IPv4 for the network type.
Make sure the Default VPC is selected.
Select the “Default” in the DB subnet group.
Step 9:
Select the Public access: Yes
For the VPC security group(firewall)- create New
Type the name of the New VPC security group - “hybrid-host-vpc”
Leave others default and Click Create database
Task 4: Create RDS in AWS console
Step 10:
Get your RDS endpoint,
After the RDS status becomes available, Click the created database 'db-hybrid-host'.
In the Connectivity&Security tab, copy the Endpoint which we will use it to connect our VM with RDS.
Task 4: Create RDS in AWS console

tasks.title: Task 5: Change the Inbound rules of RDS for VM access

steps.text: In the Search bar type “EC2” and select the “EC2”
In the sidebar, “Network & security” blade Select the “Security Groups” option
Task 5: Change the Inbound rules of RDS for VM access
There we have created the separate VPC, which was created during RDS creation, click on “hybrid-host-VPC”.
Task 5: Change the Inbound rules of RDS for VM access
Click “Edit inbound rules”
Task 5: Change the Inbound rules of RDS for VM access
Make sure your VM's Public IP exists in the inbound rules in default.
Task 5: Change the Inbound rules of RDS for VM access

Once the lab steps are completed, please click on the Validation button on the Right side panel.
This will validate the resources in the AWS account and show you whether you have completed this lab successfully or not.
Sample output:
You have successfully Signed into AWS portal
You have successfully installed Apache web server, PHP and MySQL client on the VM.
You have successfully created and configured an Amazon RDS instance (MySQL)
You have successfully connected the local Ubuntu web server with the RDS.
You have successfully developed a simple web app and stores user data into the RDS database.
You have successfully completed the hybrid deployed workflow with VM and AWS.
Sign out of the AWS Console.
Confirm that resources are working as expected and clean them respectively in the console.
You have completed this Lab.
